title: Southwestern United States

infobox.value: Core: [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") Others, depending on boundaries used: [California](/wiki/California "California") [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")

or simply **the Southwest**, is a geographic and cultural [region of the United States](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") that includes [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), along with adjacent portions of [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"). The largest cities by [metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas") are [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"), [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"), and [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Before 1848, in the historical region of [Santa Fe de Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") as well as parts of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") and [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas"), settlement was almost non-existent outside of New Mexico's [pueblos](/wiki/Pueblo "Pueblo") and [Spanish or Mexican municipalities](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico#Regions_and_municipalities "Santa Fe de Nuevo México"). Much of the area had been a part of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") and Mexico until the United States acquired the area through the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848 and the smaller [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase") in 1854.

includes only the states of Arizona and New Mexico; others focus on the land within the old Spanish and Mexican borders of the [*Nuevo México* Province](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") or the later American [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory").[[5]](#cite_note-Kessell_2013_p.-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Douglass_Graves_2017_p.-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Oliva_1993_p._242-7)

The **Southwestern United States**, also known as the **American Southwest** or simply **the Southwest**, is a geographic and cultural [region of the United States](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_the_United_States "List of regions of the United States") that includes [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") and [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), along with adjacent portions of [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"). The largest cities by [metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas") are [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"), [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"), [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"), and [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Before 1848, in the historical region of [Santa Fe de Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") as well as parts of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") and [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas"), settlement was almost non-existent outside of New Mexico's [pueblos](/wiki/Pueblo "Pueblo") and [Spanish or Mexican municipalities](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico#Regions_and_municipalities "Santa Fe de Nuevo México"). Much of the area had been a part of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") and Mexico until the United States acquired the area through the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848 and the smaller [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase") in 1854.

While the region's boundaries are not officially defined, there have been attempts to do so.[[3]](#cite_note-UAtpncd-3) One such definition is from the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") in California in the west (117° west longitude) to [Carlsbad, New Mexico](/wiki/Carlsbad,_New_Mexico "Carlsbad, New Mexico"), in the east (104° west longitude); another says that it extends from the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border") in the south to the southern areas of Colorado, Utah, and Nevada in the north (39° north latitude).[[4]](#cite_note-CarltonDef-4) In another definition, the **core Southwestern U.S.** includes only the states of Arizona and New Mexico; others focus on the land within the old Spanish and Mexican borders of the [*Nuevo México* Province](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") or the later American [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory").[[5]](#cite_note-Kessell_2013_p.-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Douglass_Graves_2017_p.-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Oliva_1993_p._242-7)

## Regional geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Regional geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/ISS_View_of_the_Southwestern_USA.JPG/250px-ISS_View_of_the_Southwestern_USA.JPG)](/wiki/File:ISS_View_of_the_Southwestern_USA.JPG)

Panoramic view of the southwestern United States

The geography of the region is mainly made up of four features: the [Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert"), [Sonoran](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert"), and [Chihuahuan Deserts](/wiki/Chihuahuan_Desert "Chihuahuan Desert"), and the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau"); although there are other geographical features as well, such as a portion of the [Great Basin Desert](/wiki/Great_Basin_Desert "Great Basin Desert"). The deserts dominate the southern and western reaches of the area, while the plateau (which is largely made up of high desert) is the main feature north of the [Mogollon Rim](/wiki/Mogollon_Rim "Mogollon Rim").[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) The two major rivers of the region are the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River"), running in the northern and western areas, and the [Rio Grande](/wiki/Rio_Grande "Rio Grande"), running in the east, north to south.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Chihuahuan_Desert_from_South_Rim_BIBE.jpg/250px-Chihuahuan_Desert_from_South_Rim_BIBE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chihuahuan_Desert_from_South_Rim_BIBE.jpg)

The Chihuahuan desert terrain mainly consists of [basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin") broken by numerous small [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range").

Formed approximately 8000 years ago, the Chihuahuan Desert is a relatively dry desert,[[21]](#cite_note-NMSU-21) although it is slightly wetter than the Sonoran Desert to the west.[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) The Chihuahuan Desert spreads across the southeastern portion of the region, covering from southeastern Arizona, across southern and central New Mexico, and the portion of western Texas included in the Southwest.[[21]](#cite_note-NMSU-21) While it is the second largest desert in the United States,[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) only a third of the desert is within the United States, with the rest in Mexico.[[22]](#cite_note-NPSCD-22) El Paso and Albuquerque are the major US cities in this desert, with other smaller cities being [Las Cruces](/wiki/Las_Cruces,_New_Mexico "Las Cruces, New Mexico") and [Roswell](/wiki/Roswell,_New_Mexico "Roswell, New Mexico") in New Mexico and [Willcox](/wiki/Willcox,_Arizona "Willcox, Arizona") in Arizona.[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20)

The elevation in the Chihuahuan Desert varies from about 1,750 to 6,000 feet (500 to 1,800 meters), as well as several larger mountain ranges within or bordering this desert including the [Black Range](/wiki/Black_Range "Black Range"), [Organ Mountains](/wiki/Organ_Mountains "Organ Mountains"), [Capitan Mountains](/wiki/Capitan_Mountains "Capitan Mountains"), [Manzano Mountains](/wiki/Manzano_Mountains "Manzano Mountains"), [Sacramento Mountains](/wiki/Sacramento_Mountains_(New_Mexico) "Sacramento Mountains (New Mexico)"), [Sandia Mountains](/wiki/Sandia_Mountains "Sandia Mountains"), [Magdalena Mountains](/wiki/Magdalena_Mountains "Magdalena Mountains"), and [Chiracahua Mountains](/wiki/Chiracahua_Mountains "Chiracahua Mountains"), plus many smaller mountain ranges contained in the area, namely the [Animas](/wiki/Animas_Mountains "Animas Mountains"), [San Andres](/wiki/San_Andres_Mountains "San Andres Mountains"), [Guadalupe Mountains](/wiki/Guadalupe_Mountains "Guadalupe Mountains"), and [Doña Ana Mountains](/wiki/Do%C3%B1a_Ana_Mountains "Doña Ana Mountains") in New Mexico; and the [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin_Mountains_(Texas) "Franklin Mountains (Texas)"), [Hueco](/wiki/Hueco_Mountains "Hueco Mountains"), and [Davis Mountains](/wiki/Davis_Mountains "Davis Mountains") in Texas. It also reaches up into the foothills of the higher ranges such as the [Black Range](/wiki/Black_Range "Black Range") and [Oscura Mountains](/wiki/Oscura_Mountains "Oscura Mountains") in New Mexico. High above the desert, these forest-covered and sometimes snow-capped mountains form [sky islands](/wiki/Sky_island "Sky island"), with radically different flora and fauna than the surrounding desert below. The sky islands also supply the surrounding desert foothills with flowing water during the spring runoff and after the summer storms of the [New Mexican monsoon season](/wiki/New_Mexican_Monsoon "New Mexican Monsoon"). The Chihuahuan is a "rain shadow" desert, formed between two mountain ranges (the Sierra Madre Occidental on the west and the Sierra Madre Oriental on the east) which block oceanic precipitation from reaching the area.[[21]](#cite_note-NMSU-21) The Chihuahuan Desert is considered the "most biologically diverse desert in the Western Hemisphere and one of the most diverse in the world," and includes more species of cacti than any other desert in the world.[[22]](#cite_note-NPSCD-22) The most prolific plants in this region are [agave](/wiki/Agave "Agave"), [yucca](/wiki/Yucca "Yucca") and [creosote bushes](/wiki/Creosote_bush "Creosote bush"),[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) in addition to the ubiquitous presence of various cacti species.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/AguaFriaNM_%28cropped%29.jpeg/250px-AguaFriaNM_%28cropped%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:AguaFriaNM_(cropped).jpeg)

Saguaro cactus in the Sonoran Desert.

When people think of the desert southwest, the landscape of the Sonoran Desert is what mostly comes to mind.[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) The Sonoran Desert makes up the southwestern portion of the Southwest; most of the desert lies in Mexico, but its United States component lies on the southeastern border of California, and the western 2/3 of southern Arizona. Rainfall averages between 4 and 12 in (100 and 300 mm) per year. Its most widely known inhabitant is the [saguaro cactus](/wiki/Saguaro_cactus "Saguaro cactus"), which is unique to the Sonoran.[[23]](#cite_note-WWF-23)[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) It is bounded on the northwest by the Mojave Desert, to the north by the Colorado Plateau and to the east by the [Arizona Mountains forests](/wiki/Arizona_Mountains_forests "Arizona Mountains forests") and the Chihuahuan Desert.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Aside from the trademark saguaro, the desert has the most diverse plant life of any desert in the world,[[23]](#cite_note-WWF-23) and includes many other species of cacti, including the organ-pipe, senita, prickly pear, barrel, fishhook, hedgehog, cholla, silver dollar, and jojoba.[[23]](#cite_note-WWF-23)[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) The portion of the Sonoran Desert which lies in the Southwestern United States is the most populated area within the region. Six of the top ten major population centers of the region are found within its borders: Phoenix, [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), [Mesa](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona"), [Chandler](/wiki/Chandler,_Arizona "Chandler, Arizona"), [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), and [Scottsdale](/wiki/Scottsdale,_Arizona "Scottsdale, Arizona"), all in Arizona. Also within its borders are [Yuma](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona") and [Prescott, Arizona](/wiki/Prescott,_Arizona "Prescott, Arizona").[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20)

The most northwest portion of the American Southwest is covered by the Mojave Desert. Bordered on the south by the Sonoran Desert and the east by the Colorado Plateau, its range within the region makes up the southeast tip of Nevada, the southwestern corner of Utah and the northwestern corner of Arizona.[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) In terms of topography, the Mojave is very similar to the Great Basin Desert, which lies just to its north.[[20]](#cite_note-ASW1-20) Within the region, Las Vegas is the most populous city; other significant population centers include [Laughlin](/wiki/Laughlin,_Nevada "Laughlin, Nevada") and [Pahrump](/wiki/Pahrump,_Nevada "Pahrump, Nevada") in Nevada, [St. George](/wiki/St._George,_Utah "St. George, Utah") and [Hurricane](/wiki/Hurricane,_Utah "Hurricane, Utah") in Utah, and [Lake Havasu City](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"), [Kingman](/wiki/Kingman,_Arizona "Kingman, Arizona"), and [Bullhead City](/wiki/Bullhead_City,_Arizona "Bullhead City, Arizona") in Arizona. The Mojave is the smallest, driest and hottest desert within the United States.[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) The Mojave gets less than 6 in (150 mm) of rain annually, and its elevation ranges from 3,000 to 6,000 feet (900 to 1,800 meters) above sea level.[[26]](#cite_note-DD1-26) The most prolific vegetation is the tall [Joshua tree](/wiki/Joshua_tree "Joshua tree"), which grow as tall as 40 ft (12 m), and are thought to live almost 1000 years.[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) Other major vegetation includes the Parry saltbush and the Mojave sage, both only found in the Mojave, as well as the creosote bush.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Delicate_arch_sunset.jpg/250px-Delicate_arch_sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Delicate_arch_sunset.jpg)

The Delicate Arch at [Arches National Park](/wiki/Arches_National_Park "Arches National Park")

The [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau") varies from the large stands of forests in the west, including the largest stand of [ponderosa pine](/wiki/Ponderosa_pine "Ponderosa pine") trees in the world, to the Mesas to the east.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Colorado Plateau consists mostly of high desert. Within the Southwest U.S. region, the Colorado is bordered to the south by the Mogollon Rim and the Sonoran Desert, to the west by the Mojave Desert, and to the east by the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"), the [Rio Grande Rift](/wiki/Rio_Grande_Rift "Rio Grande Rift") valley, and the [Llano Estacado](/wiki/Llano_Estacado "Llano Estacado").[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) The Plateau is characterized by a series of plateaus and mesas, interspersed with canyons.[[24]](#cite_note-LP-24) The most dramatic example is the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon").[[29]](#cite_note-29) But that is one of many dramatic vistas included within the Plateau, which includes spectacular lava formations, "painted" deserts, sand dunes, and badlands.[[30]](#cite_note-30) One of the most distinctive features of the Plateau is its longevity, having come into existence at least 500 million years ago.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The Plateau can be divided into six sections, three of which fall into the Southwest region. Beginning with the Navajo section forming the northern boundary of the Southwestern United States, which has shallower canyons than those in the Canyonlands section just to its north; the Navajo section is bordered to the south by the Grand Canyon section, which of course is dominated by the Grand Canyon; and the southeasternmost portion of the Plateau is the Datil section, consisting of valleys, mesas, and volcanic formations.[[32]](#cite_note-EBCP-32) Albuquerque is the most populous city often considered at the edge of this portion contained in the Southwest region, but Santa Fe, New Mexico and Flagstaff, Arizona, are also significant population centers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Four_Corners_Monument_%281%29.jpg/250px-Four_Corners_Monument_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Four_Corners_Monument_(1).jpg)

[Four Corners Monument](/wiki/Four_Corners_Monument "Four Corners Monument")

[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), Tucson, and [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") dominate the westernmost metropolitan areas in the Southwest, while [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque")-[Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") and [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas")-[Las Cruces](/wiki/Las_Cruces,_New_Mexico "Las Cruces, New Mexico") dominate the easternmost metropolitan areas.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Pueblo_Bonito_Aerial_Chaco_Canyon.jpg/250px-Pueblo_Bonito_Aerial_Chaco_Canyon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pueblo_Bonito_Aerial_Chaco_Canyon.jpg)

[Ancestral Puebloan](/wiki/Ancestral_Puebloan "Ancestral Puebloan") ruins at [Chaco Canyon](/wiki/Chaco_Culture_National_Historical_Park "Chaco Culture National Historical Park")

Human history in the Southwest begins with the arrival of the [Clovis culture](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture"), a [Paleo-Indian](/wiki/Paleo-Indian "Paleo-Indian") hunter-gatherer culture which arrived sometime around 9000 BC.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201211–12-35) This culture remained in the area for several millennia. At some point they were replaced by three great [Pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") Indian cultures: the [Ancestral Pueblo people](/wiki/Ancestral_Pueblo_people "Ancestral Pueblo people"), the [Hohokam](/wiki/Hohokam "Hohokam"), and the [Mogollon](/wiki/Mogollon_culture "Mogollon culture"), all of which existed among other surrounding cultures including the [Patayan](/wiki/Patayan "Patayan").[[36]](#cite_note-36) Maize was first cultivated in the region sometime during the early first millennium BC, but it took several hundred years for the native cultures to become dependent on it as a food source.[[37]](#cite_note-37) As their dependence on maize grew, [Pre-Columbian](/wiki/Pre-Columbian "Pre-Columbian") Indians began developing irrigation systems around 600 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[38]](#cite_note-Chaco-38)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan20126-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Map_Anasazi%2C_Hohokam_and_Mogollon_cultures-en.svg/250px-Map_Anasazi%2C_Hohokam_and_Mogollon_cultures-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Anasazi,_Hohokam_and_Mogollon_cultures-en.svg)

Map of Paleo-Indians in the American Southwest and Mexico

Archaeological evidence suggests the Ancestral Pueblo people, sometimes referred to as the Anasazi, began settling the area in approximately 1500 BC.[[40]](#cite_note-BM2-40) However, the term "Anasazi" is viewed by modern [Pueblo people](/wiki/Pueblo_people "Pueblo people") as derogatory and is increasingly disused. Eventually, the Ancestral Pueblo would spread throughout the entire northern section of the Southwest.[[41]](#cite_note-41) This culture would go through several different eras lasting from approximately 1500 BC through the middle of the 15th century AD: the [Basketmaker I](/wiki/Early_Basketmaker_II_Era "Early Basketmaker II Era"), [II](/wiki/Late_Basketmaker_II_Era "Late Basketmaker II Era"), and [III](/wiki/Basketmaker_III_Era "Basketmaker III Era") phases followed by the [Pueblo I](/wiki/Pueblo_I_Period "Pueblo I Period"), [II](/wiki/Pueblo_II_Period "Pueblo II Period"), [III](/wiki/Pueblo_III_Period "Pueblo III Period"), and [IV](/wiki/Pueblo_IV_Period "Pueblo IV Period"). As the Puebloans transitioned from a nomadic lifestyle to one based on both dry land and irrigated agriculture,[[38]](#cite_note-Chaco-38) their first domiciles were pithouses.[[40]](#cite_note-BM2-40) The [Mogollon culture](/wiki/Mogollon_culture "Mogollon culture") developed later than the Puebloan, arising in the east at around 300 BC.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Their range would eventually extend deep into what would become Mexico, and dominate the southeastern portion of the Southwest.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Their settlements would evolve over time from pit-dwellings through pueblos and ultimately incorporate cliff-dwellings. The [Hohokam](/wiki/Hohokam "Hohokam") were the last of these ancestral cultures to develop, somewhere around AD 1, but became the most populous of the three by AD 1300, despite occupying the smallest territory of the three, covering most of the southwest portion.[[44]](#cite_note-AZMNH-44) Beginning in approximately AD 600, the Hohokam began to develop an extensive series of irrigation canals;[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201222–24-45) of the three major cultures in the Southwest, only the Ancestral Puebloans of the [Chaco culture](/wiki/Chaco_culture "Chaco culture") and the Hohokam developed irrigation as a means of watering their agriculture.[[38]](#cite_note-Chaco-38)[[44]](#cite_note-AZMNH-44)

Not long after the Hohokam reached the height of their culture, all three major cultures in the Southwest began to decline for unknown reasons, although severe drought and encroachment from other peoples have been postulated. By the end of the 15th century, all three cultures had disappeared. The [modern Puebloan](/wiki/Puebloan "Puebloan") tribes of the [Isleta](/wiki/Isleta_Pueblo "Isleta Pueblo"), [Sandia](/wiki/Sandia_Pueblo "Sandia Pueblo"), [Cochiti](/wiki/Cochiti_Pueblo "Cochiti Pueblo"), [Kewa](/wiki/Kewa_Pueblo "Kewa Pueblo"), [Santa Ana](/wiki/Santa_Ana_Pueblo "Santa Ana Pueblo"), [Taos](/wiki/Taos_Pueblo "Taos Pueblo"), [Jemez](/wiki/Jemez_Pueblo "Jemez Pueblo"), [Acoma](/wiki/Acoma_Pueblo "Acoma Pueblo"), [Laguna](/wiki/Laguna_Pueblo "Laguna Pueblo"), and [Zia](/wiki/Zia_Pueblo "Zia Pueblo"), as well as the [Hopi](/wiki/Hopi "Hopi") and [Zuni peoples](/wiki/Zuni_people "Zuni people"), trace their ancestry back to the Ancestral Puebloans,[[46]](#cite_note-46) while the [Akimel O'odham](/wiki/Pima_people "Pima people") and [Tohono O'odham](/wiki/Tohono_O%27odham_people "Tohono O'odham people") claim descent from Hohokam.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The area previously occupied by the Mogollon was taken over by an unrelated tribe, the [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESkiboGravesStark2007234-48) While it is unclear whether any of the modern Indian tribes are descended from the Mogollon, some archeologists and historians believe that they mixed with Ancestral Puebloans and became part of the modern Hopi and Zuni tribes.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Oraibi.jpg/250px-Oraibi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oraibi.jpg)

Oraibi pueblo

Prior to the arrival of Europeans, the Southwestern United States was inhabited by a very large population of [American Indian](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") tribes. The area once occupied by the ancestral Puebloans became inhabited by several American Indian tribes, the most populous of which were the [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_people "Navajo people"), [Ute](/wiki/Ute_people "Ute people"), [Southern Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute"), and Hopi. The Navajo, along with the Hopi, were the earliest of the modern Indian tribes to develop in the Southwest. Around AD 1100 their culture began to develop in the [Four Corners](/wiki/Four_Corners "Four Corners") area of the region.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The Navajos [migrated](/wiki/Pre-modern_human_migration "Pre-modern human migration") from northwestern Canada and eastern [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), where the majority of [Athabaskan](/wiki/Athabaskan_languages "Athabaskan languages") speakers reside.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Ute were found over most of modern-day Utah and Colorado, as well as northern New Mexico and Arizona.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The Paiutes roamed an area which covered over 45,000 square miles of southern Nevada and California, south-central Utah, and northern Arizona.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The Hopi settled the lands of the central and western portions of northern Arizona. Their village of [Oraibi](/wiki/Oraibi,_Arizona "Oraibi, Arizona"), settled in approximately AD 1100, is, along with [Acoma Sky City](/wiki/Acoma_Pueblo "Acoma Pueblo") in New Mexico, one of the oldest continuously occupied settlements in the United States.[[54]](#cite_note-54) The Mogollon area became occupied by the Apaches and the Zuni. The Apache migrated into the American Southwest from the northern areas of North America at some point between 1200 and 1500.[[55]](#cite_note-Roberts48ff-55) They settled throughout New Mexico, eastern Arizona, northern Mexico, parts of western Texas, and southern Colorado.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The Zuni count their direct ancestry through the ancestral Puebloans. The modern-day Zuni established a culture along the [Zuni River](/wiki/Zuni_River "Zuni River") in far-eastern Arizona and western New Mexico.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Both major tribes of the O'odham tribe settled in the southern and central Arizona, in the lands once controlled by their ancestors, the Hohokam.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Arrival of Europeans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Arrival of Europeans")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Expedition_Cabeza_de_Vaca_Karte.png/250px-Expedition_Cabeza_de_Vaca_Karte.png)](/wiki/File:Expedition_Cabeza_de_Vaca_Karte.png)

Narváez expedition (1528–36)

The first European intrusion into the region came from the south. In 1539, a Jesuit Franciscan named [Marcos de Niza](/wiki/Marcos_de_Niza "Marcos de Niza") led an expedition from Mexico City which passed through eastern Arizona.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The following year [Francisco Vázquez de Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1zquez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vázquez de Coronado"), based on reports from survivors of the [Narváez expedition](/wiki/Narv%C3%A1ez_expedition "Narváez expedition") (1528–36) who had crossed eastern Texas on their way to Mexico City, led an expedition to discover the Seven Golden Cities of Cíbola.[[60]](#cite_note-NPS1-60) The 1582–3 expedition of [Antonio de Espejo](/wiki/Antonio_de_Espejo "Antonio de Espejo") explored New Mexico and eastern Arizona;[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201238-61) and this led to [Juan de Oñate](/wiki/Juan_de_O%C3%B1ate "Juan de Oñate")'s establishment of the Spanish province of [Santa Fe de Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") in 1598, with a capital founded near [Ohkay Oweenge Pueblo](/wiki/Ohkay_Owingeh,_New_Mexico "Ohkay Owingeh, New Mexico"), which he called San Juan de los Caballeros.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Oñate's party also attempted to establish a settlement in Arizona in 1599, but were turned back by inclement weather.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201238-61) In 1610, [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") was founded, making it the oldest capital in United States.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

In 1664 Juan Archuleta led an expedition into what is now Colorado, becoming the first European to enter. A second Spanish expedition was led into Colorado by Juan Ulibarrí in 1706,[[65]](#cite_note-65) during which he claimed the Colorado territory for Spain.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

From 1687 to 1691 the Jesuit priest [Eusebio Kino](/wiki/Eusebio_Kino "Eusebio Kino") established several missions in the [Santa Cruz River](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_River_(Arizona) "Santa Cruz River (Arizona)") valley;[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201241-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) and Kino further explored southern and central Arizona in 1694, during which he discovered the ruins of Casa Grande.[[60]](#cite_note-NPS1-60) Beginning in 1732, Spanish settlers began to enter the region, and the Spanish started bestowing land grants in Mexico and the Southwest US.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201242-69) In 1751, the O'odham rebelled against the Spanish incursions, but the revolt was unsuccessful. In fact, it had the exact opposite effect, for the result of the rebellion was the establishment of the [presidio](/wiki/Presidio_San_Ignacio_de_Tubac "Presidio San Ignacio de Tubac") at [Tubac](/wiki/Tubac,_Arizona "Tubac, Arizona"), the first permanent European settlement in Arizona.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201243-70)

In 1768, the Spanish created the [Provincia de las Californias](/wiki/Las_Californias_Province "Las Californias Province"), which included California and the Southwest US. Over approximately the next 50 years, the Spanish continued to explore the Southwest, and in 1776 the City of Tucson was founded when the [Presidio San Augustin del Tucson](/wiki/Presidio_San_Augustin_del_Tucson "Presidio San Augustin del Tucson") was created, relocating the presidio from Tubac.[[71]](#cite_note-brit1911-71)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201246-72)

In 1776, two Franciscan priests, Francisco Atanasio Domínguez and Silvestre Vélez de Escalante, led an [expedition](/wiki/Dominguez%E2%80%93Escalante_Expedition "Dominguez–Escalante Expedition") from Santa Fe heading to California. After passing through Colorado, they became the first Europeans to travel into what is now Utah. Their journey was halted by bad weather in October, and they turned back, heading south into Arizona before turning east back to Santa Fe.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/1846_Mitchell%27s_Map_of_Texas_Oregon_and_California_-_Geographicus_-_TXORCA-mitchell-1846.jpg/250px-1846_Mitchell%27s_Map_of_Texas_Oregon_and_California_-_Geographicus_-_TXORCA-mitchell-1846.jpg)](/wiki/File:1846_Mitchell%27s_Map_of_Texas_Oregon_and_California_-_Geographicus_-_TXORCA-mitchell-1846.jpg)

1846 map: Mexican [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") (Upper California) in pink.

In 1804 Spain divided the Provincia de las Californias, creating the province [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"), which consisted primarily of what would become California, Nevada, Arizona, Colorado, Utah and New Mexico. In 1821 Mexico achieved its independence from Spain and shortly after, in 1824, developed its [constitution](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico"), which established the Alta California territory, which was the same geographic area as the earlier Spanish province.

In 1825, Arizona was visited by its first non-Spanish Europeans, English trappers.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESheridan201252-74) In 1836, the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas"), which contained the easternmost of the Southwest United States, won its independence from Mexico. In 1845 the Republic of Texas was annexed by the United States and immediately became a state, bypassing the usual territory phase. The new state still contained portions of what would eventually become parts of other states.[[75]](#cite_note-75) In 1846, the Southwest became embroiled in the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), partly as a result of the United States' annexation of Texas. On August 18, 1846, an American force captured Santa Fe, New Mexico.[[76]](#cite_note-76) On December 16 of the same year, American forces captured Tucson, Arizona, marking the end of hostilities in the Southwest United States.[[77]](#cite_note-disturnell-1882-77) When the war ended with the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") on February 2, 1848, the United States gained control of all of present-day California, Nevada and Utah, as well as the majority of Arizona, and parts of New Mexico and Colorado (the rest of present-day Colorado, and most of New Mexico had been gained by the United States in their annexation of the Republic of Texas).[[78]](#cite_note-78) The final portion of the Southwestern United States came about through the acquisition of the southernmost parts of Arizona and New Mexico through the [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase") in 1853.[[71]](#cite_note-brit1911-71)

In 1851, [San Luis](/wiki/San_Luis,_Colorado "San Luis, Colorado") became the first European settlement in what is now Colorado.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

### Becoming states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Becoming states")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/United_States_1849-1850.png/250px-United_States_1849-1850.png)](/wiki/File:United_States_1849-1850.png)

United States 1849–1850

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/United_States_1850-1853-03.png/250px-United_States_1850-1853-03.png)](/wiki/File:United_States_1850-1853-03.png)

United States 1850–1853

Of the states of which at least a portion make up the Southwest, Texas was the first to achieve statehood. On December 29, 1845, the Republic of Texas was annexed, bypassing the status of becoming a territory, and immediately became a state.[[80]](#cite_note-TAnnexation-80) Initially, its borders included parts of what would become several other states: almost half of New Mexico, a third of Colorado, and small portions of Kansas, Oklahoma, and Wyoming.[[81]](#cite_note-comp1850-81) Texas current borders were set in the [Compromise of 1850](/wiki/Compromise_of_1850 "Compromise of 1850"), where Texas ceded land to the federal government in exchange for $10 million, which would go to paying off the debt Texas had accumulated in its war with Mexico.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Following the Mexican Cession, the lands of what had been the Mexican territory of Alta California were in flux: portions of what is now New Mexico were claimed, but never controlled, by Texas. With the Compromise of 1850, the states of Texas and California were created (Texas as a slave state, and California as a free state), as well as the [Utah Territory](/wiki/Utah_Territory "Utah Territory") and [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory").[[83]](#cite_note-83) The New Mexico Territory consisted of most of Arizona and New Mexico (excluding a strip along their southern borders), a small section of southern Colorado, and the very southern tip of Nevada;[[84]](#cite_note-84) while the Utah Territory consisted of Utah, most of Nevada, and portions of Wyoming and Colorado.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The New Mexico Territory was expanded along its southern extent, to its current border, with the signing of the Gadsden Purchase Treaty on December 30, 1853,[[71]](#cite_note-brit1911-71)[[86]](#cite_note-86) which was ratified by the U.S. Congress, with some slight alterations, in April 1854.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/1860_Colorado_Territory_map.svg/250px-1860_Colorado_Territory_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1860_Colorado_Territory_map.svg)

1860 Colorado Territory map

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Utah_Territory_evolution_animation_-_August_2011.gif/250px-Utah_Territory_evolution_animation_-_August_2011.gif)](/wiki/File:Utah_Territory_evolution_animation_-_August_2011.gif)

Utah Territory evolution 1850–1868

The [Colorado Territory](/wiki/Colorado_Territory "Colorado Territory") was organized on February 28, 1861, created out of lands then currently in the Utah, Kansas, Nebraska, and New Mexico territories.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The [Nevada Territory](/wiki/Nevada_Territory "Nevada Territory") was also organized in 1861, on March 2, with land taken from the existing Utah Territory. Initially, only the western 2/3 of what is currently the State of Nevada was included in the territory, with its boundary to the east being the [39th meridian west from Washington](/wiki/39th_meridian_west_from_Washington "39th meridian west from Washington"), and to the south the [37th parallel](/wiki/37th_parallel_north "37th parallel north").[[89]](#cite_note-89) In 1862 Nevada's eastern border shifted to the [38th meridian west from Washington](/wiki/38th_meridian_west_from_Washington "38th meridian west from Washington"), and finally to its current position at the [37th meridian west from Washington](/wiki/37th_meridian_west_from_Washington "37th meridian west from Washington") in 1866. The boundary modification in 1866 also included adding the southern triangular tip of the present-day state, taken from the Arizona Territory.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

From July 24–27, 1861 a Confederate force under the command of Lt. Colonel John Robert Baylor forced the surrender of the small Union garrison stationed at [Fort Fillmore](/wiki/Fort_Fillmore "Fort Fillmore"), near [Mesilla, New Mexico](/wiki/Mesilla,_New_Mexico "Mesilla, New Mexico"). On August 1, 1861, Baylor declared the creation of the [Arizona Territory](/wiki/Confederate_Arizona "Confederate Arizona"), and claimed it for the Confederacy, with Mesilla as its capital.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The territory, which had been formed by the portion of the existing New Mexico Territory below the 34th parallel, became official on February 14, 1862.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Wpdms_Arizona_Territory_1860_ZP.svg/250px-Wpdms_Arizona_Territory_1860_ZP.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wpdms_Arizona_Territory_1860_ZP.svg)

Confederate Arizona (outlined in blue)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Wpdms_new_mexico_territory_1866.png/250px-Wpdms_new_mexico_territory_1866.png)](/wiki/File:Wpdms_new_mexico_territory_1866.png)

Split of Arizona and New Mexico territories, in 1866, after small portion ceded to Nevada

Nevada was admitted to the Union on October 31, 1864, becoming the 36th state.[[95]](#cite_note-95) This was followed by the admittance to the Union of Colorado, which became the 38th state on August 1, 1876.[[96]](#cite_note-Colorado_Statehood_Proclamation-96) Confederate Arizona was short-lived, however. By May 1862, Confederate forces had been driven out of the region by union troops. That same month a bill was introduced into the U.S. Congress, and on February 24, 1863 [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") signed the [Arizona Organic Act](/wiki/Arizona_Organic_Act "Arizona Organic Act"), which officially created the U.S. [Territory of Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Territory "Arizona Territory"), splitting the New Mexico Territory at the 107th meridian.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Utah, as shown above, evolved out of the Utah Territory, as pieces of the original territory created in 1850 were carved out: parts were ceded to Nevada, Wyoming, and Colorado in 1861; another section to Nevada in 1862; and the final section to Nevada in 1866.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGannett1885124-100) In 1890, the LDS church issued the [1890 Manifesto](/wiki/1890_Manifesto "1890 Manifesto"), which officially banned polygamy for members of the church.[[101]](#cite_note-101) It was the last roadblock for Utah entering the Union, and on January 4, 1896, Utah was officially granted statehood, becoming the 45th state.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

In 1869, [John Wesley Powell](/wiki/John_Wesley_Powell "John Wesley Powell") led a 3-month expedition which explored the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") and the Colorado River.[[103]](#cite_note-National_Atlas_US-103) In 1875, he would publish a book describing his explorations, *Report of the Exploration of the Columbia River of the West and Its Tributaries*, which was later republished as *[The Exploration of the Colorado River and Its Canyons](/wiki/The_Exploration_of_the_Colorado_River_and_Its_Canyons "The Exploration of the Colorado River and Its Canyons")*.

In 1877 silver was discovered in southeastern Arizona. The notorious mining town of [Tombstone, Arizona](/wiki/Tombstone,_Arizona "Tombstone, Arizona") was born to service the miners.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The town would become immortalized as the scene of what is considered the greatest gunfight in the history of the Old West, the [Gunfight at the O.K. Corral](/wiki/Gunfight_at_the_O.K._Corral "Gunfight at the O.K. Corral").[[105]](#cite_note-105)

Copper was also discovered in 1877, near [Bisbee](/wiki/Bisbee,_Arizona "Bisbee, Arizona") and [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome,_Arizona "Jerome, Arizona") in Arizona, which became an important component of the economy of the Southwest. Production began in 1880 and was made more profitable by the expansion of the railroad throughout the territory during the 1880s.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Santa_Fe_Route_Map_1891.jpg/250px-Santa_Fe_Route_Map_1891.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Fe_Route_Map_1891.jpg)

The second transcontinental railroad: the "Santa Fe Route" – 1891.

The early 1880s also saw the completion of the second transcontinental railroad, which ran through the heart of the Southwest, called the "Santa Fe Route." It ran from Chicago, down through Topeka, then further south to Albuquerque, before heading almost due west through northern Arizona to Los Angeles.[[107]](#cite_note-drury-107)

The repeal of the [Sherman Silver Purchase Act](/wiki/Sherman_Silver_Purchase_Act "Sherman Silver Purchase Act") in 1893 led to the decline of the silver mining industry in the region.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In 1901, the [Santa Fe Railroad](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Railroad "Santa Fe Railroad") reached the South Rim of the Grand Canyon, opening the way for a tourism boom,[[109]](#cite_note-109) a trend led by restaurant and hotel entrepreneur [Fred Harvey](/wiki/Fred_Harvey_(entrepreneur) "Fred Harvey (entrepreneur)").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

The last two territories within the Southwest to achieve statehood were New Mexico and Arizona. By 1863, with the splitting off of the Arizona Territory, New Mexico reached its modern borders. They became states within forty days of one another. On January 6, 1912, New Mexico became the 47th state in the Union.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Arizona would shortly follow, becoming the last of the 48 contiguous United States on February 14, 1912.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Since statehood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Since statehood")]

#### Ski industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Ski industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Sandia_Peak_Ski_Area%2C_Albuquerque_AC%2C_crop1.JPG/250px-Sandia_Peak_Ski_Area%2C_Albuquerque_AC%2C_crop1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sandia_Peak_Ski_Area,_Albuquerque_AC,_crop1.JPG)

Sandia Peak Ski Area, New Mexico

The 1930s saw the beginning of the ski industry in the Southwest. Resorts were established in Colorado in areas such as [Estes Park](/wiki/Estes_Park "Estes Park"), [Gunnison](/wiki/Gunnison,_Colorado#Winter "Gunnison, Colorado"), and on [Loveland Pass](/wiki/Loveland_Pass "Loveland Pass").[[113]](#cite_note-113) New Mexico's oldest ski area is [Sandia Peak Ski Area](/wiki/Sandia_Peak_Ski_Area "Sandia Peak Ski Area") at the eastern edge of [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque "Albuquerque"), which opened to skiers in 1936.[[114]](#cite_note-SPSP_History-114)[[115]](#cite_note-All-Ski-115) At the end of the decade, in 1939, with the establishment of [Alta Ski Area](/wiki/Alta_Ski_Area "Alta Ski Area"), [Utah's skiing](/wiki/Development_of_Skiing_in_Utah "Development of Skiing in Utah") began to be developed.[[116]](#cite_note-atasixty-116)

Due to the ski conditions in the state, during WWII, the [10th Mountain Division](/wiki/10th_Mountain_Division "10th Mountain Division") established [Camp Hale](/wiki/Camp_Hale "Camp Hale") in Colorado to train elite ski troops.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

## Origins of the term and historical/cultural variations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Origins of the term and historical/cultural variations")]

While this article deals with the core definition for the American Southwest, there are many others. The various definitions can be broken down into four main categories: Historical/Archeological; Geological/Topographical; Ecological; and Cultural. In the 1930s and 1940s, many definitions of the Southwest included all or part of Texas, Oklahoma, New Mexico, Arizona, California, Colorado, and Utah. As time has gone on, the definition of the Southwest has become more solidified and more compact. For example, in 1948 the [National Geographic Society](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") defined the American Southwest as all of California, Nevada, Utah, Arizona, Colorado, and New Mexico, and the southernmost sections of Oregon, Idaho, and Wyoming, as well as parts of southwest Nebraska, western Kansas, Oklahoma, and Texas. By 1977, the Society's definition had narrowed to only the four states of Utah, Arizona, Colorado, and New Mexico; and by 1982 the portion of the Southwest in the United States, as defined by the Society, had shrunk to Arizona and New Mexico, with the southernmost strip of Utah and Colorado, as well as the Mojave and Colorado deserts in California.[[3]](#cite_note-UAtpncd-3) Other individuals who focus on Southwest studies who favored a more limited extent of the area to center on Arizona and New Mexico, with small parts of surrounding areas, include [Erna Fergusson](/wiki/Erna_Fergusson "Erna Fergusson"), [Charles Lummis](/wiki/Charles_Lummis "Charles Lummis") (who claimed to have coined the term, the Southwest), and cultural geographer [Raymond Gastil](/wiki/Raymond_Gastil "Raymond Gastil"), and ethnologist [Miguel León-Portilla](/wiki/Miguel_Le%C3%B3n-Portilla "Miguel León-Portilla").[[3]](#cite_note-UAtpncd-3)

Geographer [D. W. Meinig](/wiki/D._W._Meinig "D. W. Meinig") defines the Southwest in a very similar fashion to Reed: the portion of New Mexico west of the [Llano Estacado](/wiki/Llano_Estacado "Llano Estacado") and the portion of Arizona east of the [Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert")-[Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert") and south of the "canyon lands" and also including the [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas") district of western Texas and the southernmost part of Colorado.[[118]](#cite_note-118) Meinig breaks the Southwest down into four distinct subregions. He calls the first subregion "[Northern New Mexico](/wiki/Northern_New_Mexico "Northern New Mexico")," and describes it as focused on [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico") and [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico"). It extends from the [San Luis Valley](/wiki/San_Luis_Valley "San Luis Valley") of southern Colorado to south of [Socorro](/wiki/Socorro,_New_Mexico "Socorro, New Mexico") and including the [Sandia-Manzano Mountains](/wiki/Sandia-Manzano_Mountains "Sandia-Manzano Mountains"), with an east–west breadth in the north stretching from the upper [Canadian River](/wiki/Canadian_River "Canadian River") to the upper [San Juan River](/wiki/San_Juan_River_(Colorado_River) "San Juan River (Colorado River)"). The area around Albuquerque is sometimes called [Central New Mexico](/wiki/Central_New_Mexico "Central New Mexico").

"Central Arizona" is a vast metropolitan area spread across one contiguous sprawling oasis, essentially equivalent to the [Phoenix metropolitan area](/wiki/Phoenix_metropolitan_area "Phoenix metropolitan area"). The city of [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") is the largest urban center, and located in the approximate center of the area that includes [Tempe](/wiki/Tempe,_Arizona "Tempe, Arizona"), [Mesa](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona"), and many others.[[119]](#cite_note-119)

Meinig calls the third subregion "El Paso, Tucson, and the Southern Borderlands." While [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas") and [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona") are distinctly different cities, they serve as anchor points to the hinterland between them. [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona") occupies a large oasis at the western end of the El Paso-Tucson corridor. The region between the two cities is a major transportation trunk with settlements serving both highway and railway needs. There are also large mining operations, ranches, and agricultural oases. Both El Paso and Tucson have large military installations nearby; [Fort Bliss](/wiki/Fort_Bliss "Fort Bliss") and [White Sands Missile Range](/wiki/White_Sands_Missile_Range "White Sands Missile Range") north of El Paso in New Mexico, and, near Tucson, the [Davis-Monthan Air Force Base](/wiki/Davis-Monthan_Air_Force_Base "Davis-Monthan Air Force Base"). About 70 miles (110 km) to the southeast are the research facilities at [Fort Huachuca](/wiki/Fort_Huachuca "Fort Huachuca"). These military installations form a kind of hinterland around the El Paso-Tucson region, and are served by scientific and residential communities such as [Sierra Vista](/wiki/Sierra_Vista,_Arizona "Sierra Vista, Arizona"), [Las Cruces](/wiki/Las_Cruces,_New_Mexico "Las Cruces, New Mexico"), and [Alamogordo](/wiki/Alamogordo,_New_Mexico "Alamogordo, New Mexico"). El Paso's influence extends north into the [Mesilla Valley](/wiki/Mesilla_Valley "Mesilla Valley"), and southeast along the Rio Grande into the [Trans-Pecos](/wiki/Trans-Pecos "Trans-Pecos") region of Texas.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

The fourth subregion Meinig calls the "Northern Corridor and Navajolands," a major highway and railway trunk which connects Albuquerque and [Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona"). Just north of the transportation trunk are large blocks of American Indian land.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

### Historical/archeological

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Historical/archeological")]

See also: [Oasisamerica](/wiki/Oasisamerica "Oasisamerica")

As the [US expanded westward](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_the_United_States "Territorial evolution of the United States"), the country's [western border](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier") also shifted westward, and consequently, so did the location of the Southwestern and [Northwestern United States](/wiki/Northwestern_United_States "Northwestern United States"). In the early years of the United States, [newly colonized lands](/wiki/Overmountain_Men "Overmountain Men") lying immediately west of the Appalachian Mountains were [detached](/wiki/State_cessions "State cessions") from North Carolina and given the name [Southwest Territory](/wiki/Southwest_Territory "Southwest Territory"). During the decades that followed, the [region known as "the Southwestern United States"](/wiki/Old_Southwest "Old Southwest") covered much of the [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") east of the Mississippi River.

As territories and eventual states to the west were added after the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), the geographical "Southwest" expanded, and the relationship of these new acquisitions to the South itself became "increasingly unclear."[[122]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-122)

However, archeologist Erik Reed gives a description which is the most widely accepted as defining the American Southwest, which runs from Durango, Colorado in the north, to Durango, Mexico, in the south, and from Las Vegas, Nevada in the west to Las Vegas, New Mexico in the East. Reed's definition is roughly equivalent to the western half of the Learning Center of the American Southwest's definition, leaving out any portion of Kansas and Oklahoma, and much of Texas, as well as the eastern half of New Mexico. Since this article is about the Southwestern United States, the areas of Sonora and Chihuahua in Mexico will be excluded. The portion left includes Arizona and western New Mexico, the very southernmost part of Utah, southwestern Colorado, the very tip of west Texas, and triangle formed by the southern tip of Nevada. This will be the defined scope that is used in this article unless otherwise specified in a particular area.[[123]](#cite_note-LCASDefine-123)

### Geological/topographical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geological/topographical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/LCASMapoftheSouthwest.jpg/250px-LCASMapoftheSouthwest.jpg)](/wiki/File:LCASMapoftheSouthwest.jpg)

Map of the Southwestern United States as defined by the Learning Center of the American Southwest[[123]](#cite_note-LCASDefine-123)

Parts of the other states make up the various areas that can be included in the Southwest, depending on the source. The Learning Center of the American Southwest (LCAS)[[a]](#cite_note-124) does not rely on current state boundaries, and defines the American Southwest as parts of Arizona, Colorado, Kansas, New Mexico, Oklahoma, Texas, and Utah.[[123]](#cite_note-LCASDefine-123)
From this perspective, almost all of the region's physiographical traits, geological formations, and weather are contained within a box between 26° and 38° northern latitude, and 98° 30' and 124° western longitude.[[124]](#cite_note-UAtsd-125)

### Ecological

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ecological")]

When looking at the fauna of the region, there is a broader definition of the American Southwest. The Southwestern Center for Herpetological Research defines the Southwest as being only the states of Arizona, New Mexico, with parts of California, Nevada, Texas, and Utah; although they include all of those six states in their map of the region, solely for ease of defining the border.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

### Cultural

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Cultural")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Apache_Wickiup%2C_Edward_Curtis%2C_1903.jpg/250px-Apache_Wickiup%2C_Edward_Curtis%2C_1903.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apache_Wickiup,_Edward_Curtis,_1903.jpg)

The [Wigwam](/wiki/Wigwam "Wigwam"). A dwelling used by various Native American tribes among the Southwestern US.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Drawing_of_a_country_store_by_Marguerite_Martyn.jpg/250px-Drawing_of_a_country_store_by_Marguerite_Martyn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Drawing_of_a_country_store_by_Marguerite_Martyn.jpg)

Fanciful drawing by [Marguerite Martyn](/wiki/Marguerite_Martyn "Marguerite Martyn") in the *[St. Louis Post-Dispatch](/wiki/St._Louis_Post-Dispatch "St. Louis Post-Dispatch")* of October 21, 1906, headed "Passing of the Country Store in the Southwest"

Lawrence Clark Powell, a major bibliographer whose emphasis is on the Southwest, defined the American Southwest in a 1958 *[Arizona Highways](/wiki/Arizona_Highways "Arizona Highways")* article as, "the lands lying west of the Pecos, north of the [Mexican] Border, south of the Mesa Verde and the Grand Canyon, and east of the mountains which wall off Southern California and make it a land in itself."[[3]](#cite_note-UAtpncd-3)

Texas has long been the focal point of this dichotomy, and is often considered, as such, the *core area* of "the South's Southwest."[[122]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-122) While the [Trans-Pecos](/wiki/Trans-Pecos "Trans-Pecos") area is generally acknowledged as part of the *desert Southwest*,[[126]](#cite_note-127) most of Texas and large parts of Oklahoma are often placed into a sub-region of the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), which some consider southwestern in the general framework of the original application, meaning the "Western South." This is an area containing the basic elements of Southern [history](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Southern_United_States "Culture of the Southern United States"), [politics](/wiki/Solid_South "Solid South"), [religion](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), and [linguistic](/wiki/Southern_American_English "Southern American English") and settlement patterns, yet blended with traits of the frontier West. While this particular Southwest is notably different in many ways from the classic "Old South" or [Southeast](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States"), these features are strong enough to give it a separate southwestern identity quite different in nature from that of the interior southwestern states to the west.

One of these distinguishing characteristics in Texas—in addition to having been a [Confederate state](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") during the Civil War—is that Indigenous and Spanish American culture never played a central role in the development of this area in relative comparison to the others, as the vast majority of settlers were Anglo and blacks from the South.[[127]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-128) Although the present-day state of Oklahoma was [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") until the early 20th century, many of these American Indians were from the southeastern United States and became culturally assimilated early on. The majority of members of these tribes also allied themselves with the Confederacy during the Civil War. Combined with that, once the territory was open for settlement, southeastern pioneers made up a disproportionate number of these newcomers. All this contributed to the new state having a character that differed from other parts of the Southwest with large American Indian populations.[[127]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-128)

The fact that a majority of residents of Texas and Oklahoma—unlike those in other "southwestern" states—self-identify as living in the South and consider themselves southerners rather than the West and westerners—also lends to treating these two states as a somewhat distinct and separate entity in terms of regional classification.[[128]](#cite_note-129)
