## Vegetation and terrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Vegetation and terrain")]

Vegetation of the southwest includes various types of [yucca](/wiki/Yucca "Yucca"), along with [saguaro cactus](/wiki/Saguaro_cactus "Saguaro cactus"), [barrel cactus](/wiki/Barrel_cactus "Barrel cactus"), [prickly pear cactus](/wiki/Opuntia "Opuntia"), [desert spoon](/wiki/Desert_spoon "Desert spoon"), [creosote bush](/wiki/Creosote_bush "Creosote bush"), [sagebrush](/wiki/Sagebrush "Sagebrush"), and [greasewood](/wiki/Greasewood "Greasewood"). Many native cacti grow throughout Nevada, Utah, Colorado, and west Texas.[[129]](#cite_note-Weniger_1969_p._14-130) [Steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe") is also located all over the high plains areas in Colorado, New Mexico, and Texas. The mountains of the southwestern states have large tracts of alpine trees.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Curry_County_Eastern_New_Mexico_2010.jpg/250px-Curry_County_Eastern_New_Mexico_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Curry_County_Eastern_New_Mexico_2010.jpg)

The [High Plains](/wiki/High_Plains_(United_States) "High Plains (United States)") in [Eastern New Mexico](/wiki/Eastern_New_Mexico "Eastern New Mexico"), but also located in [Eastern Colorado](/wiki/Colorado_Eastern_Plains "Colorado Eastern Plains") and [West Texas](/wiki/West_Texas "West Texas")

Landscape features of the core southwestern areas include mountains, canyons, mesas, [buttes](/wiki/Butte "Butte"), high broad basins, plateaus, desert lands, and some plains, characteristic of the [Basin and Range Province](/wiki/Basin_and_Range_Province "Basin and Range Province"). The entire southwestern region features semi-arid to arid terrain. The far eastern part of southwestern Texas, for example, the [Texas Hill Country](/wiki/Texas_Hill_Country "Texas Hill Country"), consists of dry, tall, and rugged rocky hills of limestone and granite. [South Texas](/wiki/South_Texas "South Texas") and the [Rio Grande Valley](/wiki/Lower_Rio_Grande_Valley "Lower Rio Grande Valley") is mostly flat with many places consisting of scrub and bare topsoil, much like the deserts further west.

## Wildlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Wildlife")]

The region has an extremely diverse bird population, with hundreds of species being found in the American Southwest. In the [Chiricahua Mountains](/wiki/Chiricahua_Mountains "Chiricahua Mountains") alone, in southeastern Arizona, there can be found more than 400 species. Species include [Canada](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose") (*Branta canadensis*) and [snow geese](/wiki/Snow_goose "Snow goose"), [sandhill cranes](/wiki/Sandhill_crane "Sandhill crane") (*Grus canadensis*),[[130]](#cite_note-131) and the [roadrunner](/wiki/Greater_roadrunner "Greater roadrunner"), the state bird of New Mexico and most famous bird in the region, is found in all states of the Southwest.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Birds of prey include the [red-tailed hawk](/wiki/Red-tailed_hawk "Red-tailed hawk") (*Buteo jamaicensis*), [Cooper's hawk](/wiki/Cooper%27s_hawk "Cooper's hawk") (*Accipiter cooperii*), the [osprey](/wiki/Osprey "Osprey") (*Pandion haliaetus*), [golden eagles](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle") (*Aquila chrysaetos*), [Harris's hawk](/wiki/Harris%27s_hawk "Harris's hawk") (*Parabuteo unicinctus*),[[132]](#cite_note-133) American kestrel (*Falco sparverius*), peregrine falcon (*Falco peregrinus*),[[133]](#cite_note-134) the gray hawk (*Buteo plagiatus*),[[134]](#cite_note-135) the [American barn owl](/wiki/American_barn_owl "American barn owl") (*Tyto furcata*), the [western screech owl](/wiki/Western_screech_owl "Western screech owl") (*Megascops kennicottii*), the [great horned owl](/wiki/Great_horned_owl "Great horned owl") (*Bubo virginianus*), the [elf owl](/wiki/Elf_owl "Elf owl") (*Micrathene whitneyi*), and the [burrowing owl](/wiki/Burrowing_owl "Burrowing owl") (*Athene cunicularia*)[[135]](#cite_note-136)

Other bird species include the [turkey vulture](/wiki/Turkey_vulture "Turkey vulture") (*Cathartes aura*), the [black vulture](/wiki/Black_vulture "Black vulture") (*Coragyps atratus*),[[136]](#cite_note-137) the [northern cardinal](/wiki/Northern_cardinal "Northern cardinal") (*Cardinalis cardinalis*), the [blue grosbeak](/wiki/Blue_grosbeak "Blue grosbeak") (*Passerina caerulea*),[[137]](#cite_note-138) the [house finch](/wiki/House_finch "House finch") (*Haemorhous mexicanus*), the [lesser goldfinch](/wiki/Lesser_goldfinch "Lesser goldfinch") (*Spinus psaltria*),[[138]](#cite_note-139) the [broad-billed hummingbird](/wiki/Broad-billed_hummingbird "Broad-billed hummingbird") (*Cynanthus latirostris*), the [black-chinned hummingbird](/wiki/Black-chinned_hummingbird "Black-chinned hummingbird") (*Archilochus alexandri*), [Costa's hummingbird](/wiki/Costa%27s_hummingbird "Costa's hummingbird") (*Calypte costae*),[[139]](#cite_note-140) [Gambel's quail](/wiki/Gambel%27s_quail "Gambel's quail") (*Callipepla gambelii*),[[140]](#cite_note-141) the [common raven](/wiki/Common_raven "Common raven") (*Corvus corax*),[[141]](#cite_note-142) the [Gila woodpecker](/wiki/Gila_woodpecker "Gila woodpecker") (*Melanerpes uropygialis*), the [gilded flicker](/wiki/Gilded_flicker "Gilded flicker") (*Colaptes chrysoides*),[[142]](#cite_note-143) the [cactus wren](/wiki/Cactus_wren "Cactus wren") (*Campylorhynchus brunneicapillus*), the [rock wren](/wiki/Rock_wren "Rock wren") (*Salpinctes obsoletus*),[[143]](#cite_note-144) and the federally endangered [Southwest willow flycatcher](/wiki/Southwest_willow_flycatcher "Southwest willow flycatcher") (*Empidonax traillii extimus*).[[144]](#cite_note-145) Four types of doves call the Southwest home: the [white-winged dove](/wiki/White-winged_dove "White-winged dove") (*Zenaida asiatica*), the [mourning dove](/wiki/Mourning_dove "Mourning dove") (*Zenaida macroura*), the [common ground dove](/wiki/Common_ground_dove "Common ground dove") (*Columbina passerina*), and the [Inca dove](/wiki/Inca_dove "Inca dove") (*Columbina inca*).[[145]](#cite_note-146)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Desert_Bighorn_Sheep_Joshua_Tree_cropped.JPG/250px-Desert_Bighorn_Sheep_Joshua_Tree_cropped.JPG)](/wiki/File:Desert_Bighorn_Sheep_Joshua_Tree_cropped.JPG)

[Desert bighorn sheep](/wiki/Desert_bighorn_sheep "Desert bighorn sheep")

Mammal species include the [bobcat](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat"), [coyote](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"), [black bear](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear"), [black-tailed jackrabbit](/wiki/Black-tailed_jackrabbit "Black-tailed jackrabbit"), [desert cottontail](/wiki/Desert_cottontail "Desert cottontail"), [desert bighorn sheep](/wiki/Desert_bighorn_sheep "Desert bighorn sheep"), [mule deer](/wiki/Mule_deer "Mule deer"), [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [gray fox](/wiki/Gray_fox "Gray fox"), [mountain lion](/wiki/Cougar "Cougar"), [river otter](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter"), [long-tailed weasel](/wiki/Long-tailed_weasel "Long-tailed weasel"), [western spotted skunk](/wiki/Western_spotted_skunk "Western spotted skunk"), [pronghorn](/wiki/Pronghorn "Pronghorn"), [raccoon](/wiki/Raccoon "Raccoon"), [cactus mouse](/wiki/Cactus_mouse "Cactus mouse"), and [Ord's kangaroo rat](/wiki/Ord%27s_kangaroo_rat "Ord's kangaroo rat"), all of which can be found in parts of every southwestern state. [Elk](/wiki/Elk "Elk") are found in parts of Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, and Arizona. [White-nosed coati](/wiki/White-nosed_coati "White-nosed coati") and [collared peccary](/wiki/Collared_peccary "Collared peccary")—or *javelina*—in the Southwest are normally found in southern areas of Arizona, New Mexico, and Texas near the Mexican border. [Jaguars](/wiki/Jaguar "Jaguar") can be found in the [bootheel region](/wiki/New_Mexico_Bootheel "New Mexico Bootheel") of [Southwestern New Mexico](/wiki/Southwestern_New_Mexico "Southwestern New Mexico").[[146]](#cite_note-147) The [Mexican wolf](/wiki/Mexican_wolf "Mexican wolf") (*Canis lupus baileyi*) was reintroduced to Arizona and New Mexico in 1998.[[147]](#cite_note-paquet2003-148) A U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service study reported a minimum population of 241 Mexican wolves in southwest New Mexico and southeast Arizona at the beginning of 2023.[[148]](#cite_note-149)

There is a large contingent of snakes native to the region. Among them include the [rosy boa](/wiki/Rosy_boa "Rosy boa") (*Lichanura trivirgata*); several sub-species of the [glossy snake](/wiki/Glossy_snake "Glossy snake") (*Arizona elegans*); the [Trans-Pecos ratsnake](/wiki/Bogertophis_subocularis "Bogertophis subocularis") (*Bogertophis subocularis*); several sub-species of shovel-nosed snakes; several sub-species of kingsnake, including the [desert kingsnake](/wiki/Desert_kingsnake "Desert kingsnake") (*Lampropeltis getula splendida*) and the [Arizona mountain kingsnake](/wiki/Lampropeltis_pyromelana "Lampropeltis pyromelana") (*Lampropeltis pyromelana*); the [Arizona coral snake](/wiki/Micruroides "Micruroides") (*Micruroides euryxanthus*); the [western diamondback rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_atrox "Crotalus atrox") (*Crotalus atrox*); the [Trans-Pecos copperhead](/wiki/Agkistrodon_contortrix_pictigaster "Agkistrodon contortrix pictigaster") (*Agkistrodon contortrix pictigaster*); the [Sonoran sidewinder](/wiki/Crotalus_cerastes_cercobombus "Crotalus cerastes cercobombus") (*Crotalus cerastes cercobombus*); the [Arizona black rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_cerberus "Crotalus cerberus") (*Crotalus oreganus cerberus*); the [western rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_viridis "Crotalus viridis") (*Crotalus viridis*); the [Grand Canyon rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_oreganus_abyssus "Crotalus oreganus abyssus") (*Crotalus oreganus abyssus*), found only in Arizona; several sub-species of the [ridge-nosed rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_willardi "Crotalus willardi") (*Crotalus willardi*), the most recent rattlesnake species to be discovered in the United States, including the [New Mexico ridge-nosed rattlesnake](/wiki/Crotalus_willardi_obscurus "Crotalus willardi obscurus") (*Crotalus willardi obscurus*), and the Arizona ridge-nosed rattlesnake, the state reptile of Arizona; and the [desert massasauga](/wiki/Sistrurus_catenatus_edwardsii "Sistrurus catenatus edwardsii") (*Sistrurus catenatus edwardsii*).[[149]](#cite_note-150)

Other reptiles in the region include lizards and turtles. Lizards are highly represented in the region, the most distinctive denizen being the [Gila monster](/wiki/Gila_monster "Gila monster"), native only to the American Southwest and the state of Sonora in Mexico. The [New Mexico whiptail](/wiki/New_Mexico_whiptail "New Mexico whiptail") is the [state reptile](/wiki/List_of_state_symbols_of_New_Mexico "List of state symbols of New Mexico") of New Mexico. Other lizards include: [Sonoran collared lizard](/wiki/Sonoran_collared_lizard "Sonoran collared lizard") (*Crotaphytus nebrius*); several types of geckos, including [western banded gecko](/wiki/Western_banded_gecko "Western banded gecko") (*Coleonyx variegatus*), the [common house gecko](/wiki/Common_house_gecko "Common house gecko") (*Hemidactylus frenatus*), and the [Mediterranean house gecko](/wiki/Mediterranean_house_gecko "Mediterranean house gecko") (*Hemidactylus turcicus*), the last two species being non-native to the region but have been introduced; the [desert iguana](/wiki/Desert_iguana "Desert iguana") (*Dipsosaurus dorsalis*); the [chuckwalla](/wiki/Chuckwalla "Chuckwalla") (*Sauromalus ater*); the [greater earless lizard](/wiki/Greater_earless_lizard "Greater earless lizard") (*Cophosaurus texanus scitulus*); several sub-species of [horned lizards](/wiki/Horned_lizard "Horned lizard") (*Phrynosoma*); numerous species of [spiny lizards](/wiki/Spiny_lizard "Spiny lizard") (*Sceloporus*); [Gilbert's skink](/wiki/Gilbert%27s_skink "Gilbert's skink") (*Plestiodon gilberti*); the [western skink](/wiki/Western_skink "Western skink") (*Plestiodon skiltonianus*); [Trans-Pecos striped whiptail](/wiki/Trans-Pecos_striped_whiptail "Trans-Pecos striped whiptail") (*Aspidoscelis inornata heptagrammus*); and the [Arizona night lizard](/wiki/Arizona_night_lizard "Arizona night lizard") (*Xantusia arizonae*).[[150]](#cite_note-151) Turtles are less numerous than their other reptilian counterparts, but several are found in the region, including: the [western painted turtle](/wiki/Painted_turtle#Western_painted_turtle "Painted turtle") (*Chrysemys picta bellii*); the [Rio Grande cooter](/wiki/Rio_Grande_cooter "Rio Grande cooter") (*Pseudemys gorzugi*); the [desert box turtle](/wiki/Desert_box_turtle "Desert box turtle") (*Terrapene ornata luteola*); the [Big Bend slider](/wiki/Big_Bend_slider "Big Bend slider") (*Trachemys gaigeae gaigeae*); the [Sonora mud turtle](/wiki/Sonora_mud_turtle "Sonora mud turtle") (*Kinosternon sonoriense*); and the [desert tortoise](/wiki/Desert_tortoise "Desert tortoise") (*Gopherus agassizii*).[[151]](#cite_note-152)

Amphibians include numerous toads and frogs in the American Southwest. Toads which can be found in the region include the [Great Plains toad](/wiki/Great_Plains_toad "Great Plains toad") (*Anaxyrus cognatus*); the [green toad](/wiki/Anaxyrus_debilis "Anaxyrus debilis") (*Anaxyrus debilis*); the [Arizona toad](/wiki/Arizona_toad "Arizona toad") (*Anaxyrus microscaphus*); the [New Mexico spadefoot](/wiki/New_Mexico_spadefoot_toad "New Mexico spadefoot toad") (*Spea multiplicata stagnalis*); and the [Colorado River toad](/wiki/Colorado_River_toad "Colorado River toad") (*Incilius alvarius*), also known as the Sonoran Desert toad. Frog representation includes: [western barking frog](/wiki/Craugastor_augusti "Craugastor augusti") (*Craugastor augusti*); the [canyon tree frog](/wiki/Canyon_tree_frog "Canyon tree frog") (*Hyla arenicolor*); the [Arizona treefrog](/wiki/Wright%27s_mountain_tree_frog "Wright's mountain tree frog") (*Hyla wrightorum*); the [western chorus frog](/wiki/Western_chorus_frog "Western chorus frog") (*Pseudacris triseriata*); [Chiricahua leopard frog](/wiki/Chiricahua_leopard_frog "Chiricahua leopard frog") (*Lithobates chiricahuensis*); and the [relict leopard frog](/wiki/Relict_leopard_frog "Relict leopard frog") (*Lithobates onca*). There are quite a few salamanders throughout the region, including: the [Arizona tiger salamander](/wiki/Barred_tiger_salamander#Subspecies "Barred tiger salamander") (*Ambystoma mavortium nebulosum*) and the [painted ensatina](/wiki/Ensatina#Subspecies "Ensatina") (*Ensatina eschscholtzii picta*).[[152]](#cite_note-153)

Despite the Southwest being mostly arid, various fishes are found where water is available, including various species unique to the region. [Apache trout](/wiki/Apache_trout "Apache trout") and [Gila trout](/wiki/Gila_trout "Gila trout") are two [salmonids](/wiki/Salmonidae "Salmonidae") endemic to the area, with the former found only in Arizona and the latter only in Arizona and New Mexico.[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155) Desert [pupfishes](/wiki/Pupfish "Pupfish") are several closely related species of fish in the genus Cyprinodon, many of which are found in isolated spring-fed ponds hundreds of miles from each other, ranging from far West Texas to [Death Valley](/wiki/Death_Valley "Death Valley") in California. These pupfishes often thrive in water considerably higher in temperature and dissolved solids than most fish can tolerate.[[155]](#cite_note-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157) Many of these desert fish species are endangered due to their limited and tenuous habitat, as well as loss of habitat due to human consumption of groundwater and diversion of surface water, as well as the introduction of species such as sportfish for recreation (see: [Rio Grande Silvery Minnow v. Bureau of Reclamation](/wiki/Rio_Grande_Silvery_Minnow_v._Bureau_of_Reclamation "Rio Grande Silvery Minnow v. Bureau of Reclamation")).

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Sonoradesert_1.JPG/250px-Sonoradesert_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sonoradesert_1.JPG)

[Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert") terrain near [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona")

The southwestern United States features a semi-arid to arid climate, depending on the location.[[157]](#cite_note-BATTG19-158) Much of the Southwest is an arid desert climate, but higher elevations in the mountains in each state, with the exception of West Texas, feature alpine climates with very large amounts of snow. The metropolitan areas of Phoenix, Tucson, Las Vegas, and El Paso hardly ever receive any snow at all, as they are strictly desert lands with mountains.[[157]](#cite_note-BATTG19-158) Albuquerque receives less snow than other cities, but still receives significant snowfalls occasionally in the winter. Although it snows in this region, the snow in this part of the United States melts rapidly, often before nightfall. This is due mainly to the higher altitude and abundant sunshine in these states.

Nevada and Arizona are both generally arid with desert lands and mountains, and receive large amounts of snow in the higher elevations in and near the mountains. New Mexico, Utah, and Colorado are generally arid, with desert lands and mountains as well. They all receive decent amounts of snow and large amounts of snow in the high elevations in the mountains, although some areas in far southwestern and southern New Mexico do not receive much snow at all at lower elevations. West Texas is generally arid as well but does not receive the same amount of snow that the other southwestern states receive at their high elevations. The terrain of western Texas in the Southwest is the flat, rolling land of the plains, which eventually turns into a desert with some hills. There are significant mountains as well in west Texas upon reaching the [Trans-Pecos](/wiki/Trans-Pecos "Trans-Pecos") area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Carlsbad_caverns_entrance.jpg/250px-Carlsbad_caverns_entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carlsbad_caverns_entrance.jpg)

[Chihuahuan Desert](/wiki/Chihuahuan_Desert "Chihuahuan Desert") terrain near [Carlsbad](/wiki/Carlsbad,_New_Mexico "Carlsbad, New Mexico")

The term "High Desert" is synonymous with this region. The High Desert is generally defined as the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert") and the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau"),[[158]](#cite_note-PBS-159) which extends from inland southern California into southern Nevada, east to the [Rio Grande Rift](/wiki/Rio_Grande_Rift "Rio Grande Rift") in New Mexico. The High Desert also extends into parts of the [Northwest](/wiki/Northwestern_United_States "Northwestern United States"), such as the [Red Desert](/wiki/Red_Desert_(Wyoming) "Red Desert (Wyoming)") in southwestern Wyoming. The High Desert is very different from the lower desert lands found in Arizona, in the [Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert "Sonoran Desert"). This area of the desert land generally sits at a very high elevation, much higher than the normal desert land, and can receive very cold temperatures at night in the winter (with the exception of California, southern Nevada and southwestern Utah), sometimes near zero degrees on very cold nights. The High Desert also receives a decent amount of snowfall in the winter (with the exception of California, southern Nevada and southwestern Utah) which melts very quickly. Rain falls in this region mainly in the summer, during the [North American Monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon") season.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/CONM_Independence_monument_2.jpg/250px-CONM_Independence_monument_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:CONM_Independence_monument_2.jpg)

Monument Canyon, some of the high desert lands found in Colorado

The desert lands found in Eastern Utah, Northern Arizona, Colorado and New Mexico are usually referred to as the high desert. Colorado has scattered desert lands found in southern, southwestern, western, and northwestern parts of the state. These scattered desert lands are located in and around areas such as, the [Roan Plateau](/wiki/Roan_Plateau "Roan Plateau"), [Dinosaur National Monument](/wiki/Dinosaur_National_Monument "Dinosaur National Monument"), [Colorado National Monument](/wiki/Colorado_National_Monument "Colorado National Monument"), [Royal Gorge](/wiki/Royal_Gorge "Royal Gorge"), [Cortez](/wiki/Cortez,_Colorado "Cortez, Colorado"), [Dove Creek](/wiki/Dove_Creek,_Colorado "Dove Creek, Colorado"), [Canyons of the Ancients National Monument](/wiki/Canyons_of_the_Ancients_National_Monument "Canyons of the Ancients National Monument"), [Four Corners Monument](/wiki/Four_Corners_Monument "Four Corners Monument"), [Montrose](/wiki/Montrose,_Colorado "Montrose, Colorado"), [Blue Mesa Reservoir](/wiki/Blue_Mesa_Reservoir "Blue Mesa Reservoir"), [Pueblo](/wiki/Pueblo,_Colorado "Pueblo, Colorado"), [San Luis Valley](/wiki/San_Luis_Valley "San Luis Valley"), [Great Sand Dunes](/wiki/Great_Sand_Dunes_National_Park_and_Preserve "Great Sand Dunes National Park and Preserve") and [Joshua Tree National Park](/wiki/Joshua_Tree_National_Park "Joshua Tree National Park"). Besides the [Chihuahuan Desert](/wiki/Chihuahuan_Desert "Chihuahuan Desert"), lands in southwestern and southern New Mexico, they also have scattered desert lands in the northwestern and northern portions of their state, which is referred to as the high desert.

During [El Niño](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o "El Niño"), winters and springs are generally colder and wetter across southern portions of the region, while the northern portion stays warmer and drier due to a southern jet stream. Under [La Niña](/wiki/La_Ni%C3%B1a "La Niña"), the opposite happens, meaning the cool and wet weather tends to stay farther north.[[157]](#cite_note-BATTG19-158) The Southwest also experiences multi-year and multi-decade episodes of severe drought, including the ongoing [southwestern North American megadrought](/wiki/Southwestern_North_American_megadrought "Southwestern North American megadrought") which emerged starting year 2000.[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161)

## National parks, monuments and forests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: National parks, monuments and forests")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Grand_Canyon_Ao%C3%BBt_2006.jpg/960px-Grand_Canyon_Ao%C3%BBt_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Canyon_Ao%C3%BBt_2006.jpg)

[Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") from the South Rim

The southwestern United States contains many well-known national parks including [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park") in Arizona, [Death Valley](/wiki/Death_Valley_National_Park "Death Valley National Park") in California, [Great Sand Dunes](/wiki/Great_Sand_Dunes_National_Park_and_Preserve "Great Sand Dunes National Park and Preserve") in Colorado, [Arches](/wiki/Arches_National_Park "Arches National Park") in Utah, [Big Bend](/wiki/Big_Bend_National_Park "Big Bend National Park") in Texas, [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin_National_Park "Great Basin National Park") in Nevada, and [White Sands](/wiki/White_Sands_National_Park "White Sands National Park") in New Mexico.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Dunes_as_White_Sands_NM.jpg/250px-Dunes_as_White_Sands_NM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dunes_as_White_Sands_NM.jpg)

[White Sands National Park](/wiki/White_Sands_National_Park "White Sands National Park"), New Mexico

Arizona parks and monuments include Grand Canyon, [Monument Valley](/wiki/Monument_Valley "Monument Valley") (a [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") park), [Petrified Forest](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park"), and [Saguaro](/wiki/Saguaro_National_Park "Saguaro National Park") national parks; the national monuments of [Agua Fria](/wiki/Agua_Fria_National_Monument "Agua Fria National Monument"), [Canyon de Chelly](/wiki/Canyon_de_Chelly_National_Monument "Canyon de Chelly National Monument"), [Casa Grande Ruins](/wiki/Casa_Grande_Ruins_National_Monument "Casa Grande Ruins National Monument"), [Chiricahua](/wiki/Chiricahua_National_Monument "Chiricahua National Monument"), [Ironwood Forest](/wiki/Ironwood_Forest_National_Monument "Ironwood Forest National Monument"), [Montezuma Castle](/wiki/Montezuma_Castle_National_Monument "Montezuma Castle National Monument"), [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_National_Monument "Navajo National Monument"), [Organ Pipe Cactus](/wiki/Organ_Pipe_Cactus_National_Monument "Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument"), [Pipe Spring](/wiki/Pipe_Spring_National_Monument "Pipe Spring National Monument"), [Sonoran Desert](/wiki/Sonoran_Desert_National_Monument "Sonoran Desert National Monument"), [Sunset Crater](/wiki/Sunset_Crater_Volcano_National_Monument "Sunset Crater Volcano National Monument"), [Tonto](/wiki/Tonto_National_Monument "Tonto National Monument"), [Tuzigoot](/wiki/Tuzigoot_National_Monument "Tuzigoot National Monument"), [Vermilion Cliffs](/wiki/Vermilion_Cliffs_National_Monument "Vermilion Cliffs National Monument"), [Walnut Canyon](/wiki/Walnut_Canyon_National_Monument "Walnut Canyon National Monument"), and [Wupatki](/wiki/Wupatki_National_Monument "Wupatki National Monument"). Other federal areas include the [Apache–Sitgreaves National Forests](/wiki/Apache%E2%80%93Sitgreaves_National_Forests "Apache–Sitgreaves National Forests") and [Tumacacori National Historical Park](/wiki/Tumacacori_National_Historical_Park "Tumacacori National Historical Park").

Southern California parks and monuments include Death Valley and [Joshua Tree](/wiki/Joshua_Tree_National_Park "Joshua Tree National Park") national parks; the national monuments of [Castle Mountains](/wiki/Castle_Mountains_National_Monument "Castle Mountains National Monument"), [Mojave Trails](/wiki/Mojave_Trails_National_Monument "Mojave Trails National Monument"), [Sand to Snow](/wiki/Sand_to_Snow_National_Monument "Sand to Snow National Monument"), and [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains_National_Monument "San Gabriel Mountains National Monument"); and [Mojave National Preserve](/wiki/Mojave_National_Preserve "Mojave National Preserve").

Colorado parks and monuments include Great Sand Dunes, [Black Canyon of the Gunnison](/wiki/Black_Canyon_of_the_Gunnison_National_Park "Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park"), and [Mesa Verde](/wiki/Mesa_Verde_National_Park "Mesa Verde National Park") national parks; the national monuments of [Browns Canyon](/wiki/Browns_Canyon_National_Monument "Browns Canyon National Monument"), [Canyons of the Ancients](/wiki/Canyons_of_the_Ancients_National_Monument "Canyons of the Ancients National Monument"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado_National_Monument "Colorado National Monument"), [Hovenweep](/wiki/Hovenweep_National_Monument "Hovenweep National Monument"), and [Yucca House](/wiki/Yucca_House_National_Monument "Yucca House National Monument"). Other federal areas include [Curecanti National Recreation Area](/wiki/Curecanti_National_Recreation_Area "Curecanti National Recreation Area") and [Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site](/wiki/Bent%27s_Old_Fort_National_Historic_Site "Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site"); as well as the national forests of [San Isabel](/wiki/San_Isabel_National_Forest "San Isabel National Forest"), [San Juan](/wiki/San_Juan_National_Forest "San Juan National Forest"), and [Uncompahgre](/wiki/Uncompahgre_National_Forest "Uncompahgre National Forest").

[Little Finland](/wiki/Little_Finland "Little Finland") in [Gold Butte National Monument](/wiki/Gold_Butte_National_Monument "Gold Butte National Monument"), Nevada

Nevada has one national park at Great Basin, and the national monuments of [Basin and Range](/wiki/Basin_and_Range_National_Monument "Basin and Range National Monument"), [Gold Butte](/wiki/Gold_Butte_National_Monument "Gold Butte National Monument"), and [Tule Springs Fossil Beds](/wiki/Tule_Springs_Fossil_Beds_National_Monument "Tule Springs Fossil Beds National Monument"). Other federal areas include [Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest](/wiki/Humboldt-Toiyabe_National_Forest "Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest"), [Lake Mead National Recreation Area](/wiki/Lake_Mead_National_Recreation_Area "Lake Mead National Recreation Area"), and [Red Rock Canyon National Conservation Area](/wiki/Red_Rock_Canyon_National_Conservation_Area "Red Rock Canyon National Conservation Area").

New Mexico has two national parks, at [Carlsbad Caverns](/wiki/Carlsbad_Caverns_National_Park "Carlsbad Caverns National Park") and White Sands. National monuments include [Aztec Ruins](/wiki/Aztec_Ruins_National_Monument "Aztec Ruins National Monument"), [Bandelier](/wiki/Bandelier_National_Monument "Bandelier National Monument"), [El Malpais](/wiki/El_Malpais_National_Monument "El Malpais National Monument"), [El Morro](/wiki/El_Morro_National_Monument "El Morro National Monument"), [Gila Cliff Dwellings](/wiki/Gila_Cliff_Dwellings_National_Monument "Gila Cliff Dwellings National Monument"), [Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks](/wiki/Kasha-Katuwe_Tent_Rocks_National_Monument "Kasha-Katuwe Tent Rocks National Monument"), [Organ Mountains–Desert Peaks](/wiki/Organ_Mountains%E2%80%93Desert_Peaks_National_Monument "Organ Mountains–Desert Peaks National Monument"), [Petroglyph](/wiki/Petroglyph_National_Monument "Petroglyph National Monument"), [Rio Grande del Norte](/wiki/Rio_Grande_del_Norte_National_Monument "Rio Grande del Norte National Monument"), and [Salinas Pueblo Missions](/wiki/Salinas_Pueblo_Missions_National_Monument "Salinas Pueblo Missions National Monument"). Other federal park areas include [Chaco Culture National Historical Park](/wiki/Chaco_Culture_National_Historical_Park "Chaco Culture National Historical Park"), [Pecos National Historical Park](/wiki/Pecos_National_Historical_Park "Pecos National Historical Park"), [Sevilleta National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Sevilleta_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Sevilleta National Wildlife Refuge"), and the national forests of [Apache](/wiki/Apache_National_Forest "Apache National Forest"), [Carson](/wiki/Carson_National_Forest "Carson National Forest"), [Gila](/wiki/Gila_National_Forest "Gila National Forest"), [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_National_Forest "Lincoln National Forest"), and [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe_National_Forest "Santa Fe National Forest").

West Texas has two national parks, at Big Bend and [Guadalupe Mountains](/wiki/Guadalupe_Mountains_National_Park "Guadalupe Mountains National Park"). Other federal park areas include [Chamizal National Memorial](/wiki/Chamizal_National_Memorial "Chamizal National Memorial") and [Fort Davis National Historic Site](/wiki/Fort_Davis_National_Historic_Site "Fort Davis National Historic Site").

National monuments include [Bears Ears](/wiki/Bears_Ears_National_Monument "Bears Ears National Monument"), [Cedar Breaks](/wiki/Cedar_Breaks_National_Monument "Cedar Breaks National Monument"), [Grand Staircase–Escalante](/wiki/Grand_Staircase%E2%80%93Escalante_National_Monument "Grand Staircase–Escalante National Monument"), Hovenweep (also in Colorado), [Natural Bridges](/wiki/Natural_Bridges_National_Monument "Natural Bridges National Monument"), and [Rainbow Bridge](/wiki/Rainbow_Bridge_National_Monument "Rainbow Bridge National Monument"). ## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Hispanic Americans can be found in large numbers in every major city in the Southwest such as [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas") (80%), [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio,_Texas "San Antonio, Texas") (63%), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") (48%), [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico") (47%), [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") (43%), [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona") (41%), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada") (32%), and [Mesa](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona") (27%). Over 60 percent of the Latino population in the Southwest is [Mexican American](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans").[[161]](#cite_note-162)

Very large Hispanic American populations can also be found in the smaller cities such as [Eagle Pass](/wiki/Eagle_Pass,_Texas "Eagle Pass, Texas") (96%), [Las Cruces](/wiki/Las_Cruces,_New_Mexico "Las Cruces, New Mexico") (56%), [Yuma](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona") (55%), [Blythe](/wiki/Blythe,_California "Blythe, California") (53%), [Pueblo](/wiki/Pueblo,_Colorado "Pueblo, Colorado") (48%), [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") (48%), and [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona") (36%). Many small towns throughout the southwestern states also have significantly large Latino populations.

The largest Asian American populations in the southwest can be found in [California](/wiki/California "California") and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"),[[162]](#cite_note-163) with some significant Asian population in Phoenix. The most significant American Indian populations can be found in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") and [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona").

More than 20% of Native Americans live in the Southwest.[[163]](#cite_note-164)

Before 1700, the only permanent Spanish settlements in the Southwest were along the valley of the upper Rio Grande in New Mexico.[[164]](#cite_note-165)

## Cities and urban areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cities and urban areas")]

The area also contains many of the nation's largest cities and metropolitan areas, despite relatively low population density in rural areas. [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") is the fifth most populous city in the country, and [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque,_New_Mexico "Albuquerque, New Mexico") and [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") were some of the fastest-growing cities in the United States.[[165]](#cite_note-m1-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167) Also, the region as a whole has witnessed some of the highest population growth in the United States, and according to the [US Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), in 2008–2009, [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") was the fastest-growing state in America. As of the 2010 Census, [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") was the fastest-growing state in the United States, with an increase of 35.1% in the last ten years. Additionally, Arizona (24.6%), Utah (23.8%), Texas (20.6%), and Colorado (16.9%) were all in the top ten fastest-growing states as well.[[167]](#cite_note-168)

The largest [metropolitan areas](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_statistical_areas "List of metropolitan statistical areas") are centered around [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_metropolitan_area "Phoenix metropolitan area") (with an estimated population of more than 5 million as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit)), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas%E2%80%93Paradise,_NV_MSA "Las Vegas–Paradise, NV MSA") (more than 2.2 million), [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona") (more than 1 million), [Albuquerque](/wiki/Albuquerque_metropolitan_area "Albuquerque metropolitan area") (more than 900,000), and [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso_metropolitan_area "El Paso metropolitan area") (more than 840,000).[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Those five metropolitan areas have an estimated total population of more than 9.6 million as of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit), with nearly 60 percent of them living in the two [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") cities—Phoenix and Tucson.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sports")]

### Professional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Professional")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Josh_Jacobs_Raiders-WFT_DEC2021_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Josh_Jacobs_Raiders-WFT_DEC2021_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Josh_Jacobs_Raiders-WFT_DEC2021_(cropped).jpg)

Runningback [Josh Jacobs](/wiki/Josh_Jacobs "Josh Jacobs") of the [Las Vegas Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders") [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") team

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/T._J._McFarland_April_29%2C_2018_%2850121467337%29.jpg/250px-T._J._McFarland_April_29%2C_2018_%2850121467337%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:T._J._McFarland_April_29,_2018_(50121467337).jpg)

[T. J. McFarland](/wiki/T._J._McFarland "T. J. McFarland") pitcher for the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") professional baseball team.

Of the four [major professional sports](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada"), Phoenix and Las Vegas are the only metropolitan areas in the Southwest that have representatives. Las Vegas is home to the [Las Vegas Raiders](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Raiders "Las Vegas Raiders") [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL") football team and the [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights") [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") hockey team. Phoenix is home to the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"), [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), and the [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") in the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"). The Greater Phoenix area is home to the [Cactus League](/wiki/Cactus_League "Cactus League"), one of two spring training leagues for Major League Baseball; fifteen of MLB's thirty teams are now included in the Cactus League.[[169]](#cite_note-170) The region has also been the scene of several NFL super bowls. Sun Devil Stadium in Tempe held [Super Bowl XXX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXX "Super Bowl XXX") in 1996, when the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") defeated the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers").[[170]](#cite_note-171) State Farm Stadium in Glendale, Arizona hosted [Super Bowl XLII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLII "Super Bowl XLII") on February 3, 2008, in which the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") defeated the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"),[[171]](#cite_note-172) as well as [Super Bowl XLIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIX "Super Bowl XLIX"), which resulted in the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") defeating the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") 28–24. The U.S. Airways Center hosted both the [1995](/wiki/1995_NBA_All-Star_Game "1995 NBA All-Star Game") and the [2009 NBA All-Star Games](/wiki/2009_NBA_All-Star_Game "2009 NBA All-Star Game").[[172]](#cite_note-173)

In 1997, the [Phoenix Mercury](/wiki/Phoenix_Mercury "Phoenix Mercury") were one of the original eight teams to launch the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA).[[173]](#cite_note-174) [Indoor American football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") is represented by the [Arizona Rattlers](/wiki/Arizona_Rattlers "Arizona Rattlers") located in Phoenix.[[174]](#cite_note-175) The region is also host to several major professional golf events: the [LPGA](/wiki/LPGA "LPGA")'s [Founder's Cup](/wiki/RR_Donnelley_LPGA_Founders_Cup "RR Donnelley LPGA Founders Cup");[[175]](#cite_note-176) the [Phoenix Open](/wiki/Phoenix_Open "Phoenix Open") and the [Shriners Hospitals for Children Open](/wiki/Shriners_Hospitals_for_Children_Open "Shriners Hospitals for Children Open") (in Las Vegas) of the [PGA](/wiki/Professional_Golfers%27_Association_of_America "Professional Golfers' Association of America");[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-178) and the [Tucson Conquistadores Classic](/wiki/Tucson_Conquistadores_Classic "Tucson Conquistadores Classic") (in Tucson), and the [Charles Schwab Cup Championship](/wiki/Charles_Schwab_Cup_Championship "Charles Schwab Cup Championship") (in Scottsdale) on the [Champions Tour](/wiki/Champions_Tour "Champions Tour") of the PGA.[[178]](#cite_note-179)[[179]](#cite_note-180)

[NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") has two venues within the region: The [Phoenix International Raceway](/wiki/Phoenix_International_Raceway "Phoenix International Raceway"), was built in 1964 with a one-mile oval, with a one-of-a-kind design, as well as a 2.5-mile road course,[[180]](#cite_note-181) and the [Las Vegas Motor Speedway](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Motor_Speedway "Las Vegas Motor Speedway"), a 1,200-acre (490 ha) complex of multiple tracks for motorsports racing.[[181]](#cite_note-182) There are several nationally recognized running events in the region, including The Phoenix Marathon, a qualifier for the [Boston Marathon](/wiki/Boston_Marathon "Boston Marathon"),[[182]](#cite_note-183) and the [Rock 'n' Roll Marathon Series](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_Marathon_Series "Rock 'n' Roll Marathon Series") in both [Phoenix](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_Arizona_Marathon "Rock 'n' Roll Arizona Marathon") and [Las Vegas](/wiki/Rock_%27n%27_Roll_Las_Vegas_Marathon "Rock 'n' Roll Las Vegas Marathon").[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185) Las Vegas is also the end point for the annual Baker to Vegas Challenge Cup Relay, a 120-mile-long foot race by law enforcement teams from around the world,[[185]](#cite_note-186) which is the largest law enforcement athletic event in the world.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

The [Professional Bull Riders](/wiki/Professional_Bull_Riders "Professional Bull Riders") association has its headquarters in Pueblo, Colorado. The [PBR World Finals](/wiki/Professional_Bull_Riders#World_Finals_Event_Champions "Professional Bull Riders") are held annually in Las Vegas,[[187]](#cite_note-188) which also hosts the [National Finals Rodeo](/wiki/National_Finals_Rodeo "National Finals Rodeo").

Since the 1950s, Las Vegas has been host to many of professional boxing's largest events, beginning with the Heavyweight non-title bout in 1955 between world light heavyweight champion [Archie Moore](/wiki/Archie_Moore "Archie Moore") and perennial contender [Niño Valdés](/wiki/Ni%C3%B1o_Vald%C3%A9s "Niño Valdés").[[188]](#cite_note-189) [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") fought his last world title bout in Las Vegas against [Larry Holmes](/wiki/Larry_Holmes "Larry Holmes") in 1980, and [Floyd Mayweather](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather "Floyd Mayweather") fought many of his major fights there.

### College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southwestern_United_States&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: College")]

The Southwest is home to a rich tradition of college sports. The [Big-12 Conference](/wiki/Big_12_Conference "Big 12 Conference") has two teams in the region, the [Arizona State Sun Devils](/wiki/Arizona_State_Sun_Devils "Arizona State Sun Devils") and the [University of Arizona Wildcats](/wiki/Arizona_Wildcats "Arizona Wildcats"). The [Mountain West Conference](/wiki/Mountain_West_Conference "Mountain West Conference") also has two teams, the [UNLV Rebels](/wiki/UNLV_Rebels "UNLV Rebels") and the [University of New Mexico Lobos](/wiki/New_Mexico_Lobos "New Mexico Lobos"). [Conference USA](/wiki/Conference_USA "Conference USA") is represented by the [University of Texas at El Paso Miners](/wiki/UTEP_Miners "UTEP Miners"). The [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference") has two teams: the [Lumberjacks](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_Lumberjacks "Northern Arizona Lumberjacks") of Northern Arizona University in Flagstaff, Arizona, and the [Southern Utah University Thunderbirds](/wiki/Southern_Utah_Thunderbirds "Southern Utah Thunderbirds") in Cedar City, Utah. The [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference") also has two representatives, the [New Mexico State University Aggies](/wiki/New_Mexico_State_Aggies "New Mexico State Aggies") in Las Cruces, New Mexico, and the [Grand Canyon University Antelopes](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_Antelopes "Grand Canyon Antelopes") in Phoenix.

Las Vegas is becoming the nexus for NCAA league basketball tournaments. The [Mountain West Conference](/wiki/Mountain_West_Conference "Mountain West Conference"), the [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference"), the [West Coast Conference](/wiki/West_Coast_Conference "West Coast Conference"), and the [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference") all hold their conference basketball tournaments in Las Vegas.[[189]](#cite_note-190)

The Southwest is the site of six college football bowl games: the Rate Bowl, formerly known as the [Cheez-it Bowl](/wiki/Insight_Bowl "Insight Bowl"), in Phoenix;[[190]](#cite_note-191) the [Arizona Bowl](/wiki/Arizona_Bowl "Arizona Bowl") in Tucson; the [Fiesta Bowl](/wiki/Fiesta_Bowl "Fiesta Bowl"), played at the University of Phoenix Stadium;[[191]](#cite_note-192) the [Las Vegas Bowl](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Bowl "Las Vegas Bowl");[[192]](#cite_note-193) the [New Mexico Bowl](/wiki/New_Mexico_Bowl "New Mexico Bowl") in Albuquerque;[[193]](#cite_note-194) and the [Sun Bowl](/wiki/Sun_Bowl "Sun Bowl") in El Paso, Texas.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

The erstwhile [20th century] [Southwest Conference](/wiki/Southwest_Conference "Southwest Conference") might seem to have been named after this region, but it had no teams from [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona") nor [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"). All but one of its teams were from schools in [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas").[[195]](#cite_note-SWC-196)
